30" Hardwood has a large conjoined oak with significant structural defects, including extensive internal decay, a large trunk cavity, included bark between stems, and evidence of previous damage and failure.Reason for Removal:Diseased
		
		
			Tree number:2Species:HardwoodTree Size (DBH):8Tree location:front center - marked with ribbonDescription of Tree:The approximately 8-inch hardwood stub has a severe longitudinal trunk split with a large open cavity extending through much of the stem. The defect appears to be associated with advanced decay and loss of structural wood, leaving only narrow strips of intact wood along portions of the trunk. The tree is also heavily overgrown with vines, which obscure the extent of the damage and may contribute additional loading. Given the significant decay, open seam, and compromised trunk structure, this tree is considered a hazard. Removal is recommended to eliminate the risk of failure and protect nearby volunteers, residents, and structures.Reason for Removal:Dying tree

mature red maple showing signs of possible upcoming structural failure at or near the base.Reason for Removal:Dying treeComments:We don't want to lose this tree but were advised by an arborist it is dying as evidenced by the basal fungus and rot. It leans towards the house where our child sleeps, and my wife has been worried about it falling for years. It has storm damage about 20 feet up from more than 9 years ago (before we bought the property). We love trees and never like to see one go, but a dying tree potentially threatening the house is different. I would love to be wrong about this tree, but can you tell if it is in good shape still?
